<div class="blog-header">

# Schelling Point: Cooperating Without Communicating

<div class="blog-subtitle">

People who can’t communicate can cooperate by anticipating the other
person’s actions

</div>

<div class="podcast-container style-1">

<div class="audio-player-container">

<div class="controls">

<a href="#" class="not-smooth play-pause-btn"></a>

<div class="date">

Jul 12 2019

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div class="content">

**Nivi:** <span style="">Let’s talk about Schelling points.</span>

**Naval:** <span style="">The </span>[<span style="">Schelling
point</span>](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/yJfBzcDL9fBHJfZ6P/nash-equilibria-and-schelling-points)<span style="">
is a game theory concept made famous by Thomas Schelling in his book,
</span>[*<span style="">The Strategy of
Conflict</span>*](https://books.google.co.id/books?id=7RkL4Z8Yg5AC&printsec=frontcover&dq=The+Strategy+of+Conflict&hl=en&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwin7MHuw6njAhX58HMBHe2OC3YQ6AEIKjAA#v=onepage&q=The%20Strategy%20of%20Conflict&f=false)<span style="">,
which I recommend.</span>

<span style="">It’s about multiplayer games where people respond based
on what they think the other person’s response will be. He came up with
a mathematical formalization to answer: How do you get people who cannot
communicate with each other to coordinate?</span>

<span id="more-28493318"></span>

**Use social norms to cooperate when you can’t communicate**

<span style="">Suppose I want to meet with you, but I don’t tell you
where or when to meet. You also want to meet with me, but we can’t
communicate. That sounds like an impossible problem to solve—we can’t do
it. But not quite. </span>

<span style="">You can use social norms to converge on a Schelling
point. I know you’re rational and educated. And you know I’m rational
and educated. We’re both going to start thinking.</span>

*<span style="">When will we meet? </span>*<span style="">If we have to
pick an arbitrary date, we’ll probably pick New Year’s Eve.
</span>*<span style="">What time will we meet?</span>*<span style="">
Midnight or 12:01 a.m. </span>*<span style="">Where will we
meet?</span>*<span style=""> If we’re Americans, the big meeting spot is
probably New York City, the most important city.
</span>*<span style="">Where in New York City will we
meet?</span>*<span style=""> Probably under the clock at Grand Central
Station. Maybe you end up at the Empire State Building, but not
likely.</span>

**You can find Schelling points in business, art and politics**

<span style="">There are many games—whether it’s business or art or
politics—where you can find a Schelling point. So you can cooperate with
the other person, even when you can’t communicate. </span>

<span style="">Here’s a simple example: Suppose two companies are
competing heavily and hold an oligopoly. Let’s say the price fluctuates
between \$8 and \$12 for whatever the service is. Don’t be surprised if
they converge on \$10 without ever talking to each other.</span>

<div class="yarpp yarpp-related yarpp-related-website yarpp-template-yarpp-template-custom">

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>

</div>
